landing.statement:
I shape
what gets built,
and *why.*
landing.intro: 11+ years shaping how millions discover, trust and stay with products — currently leading **Growth Design for Microsoft Edge**, part of [[Microsoft AI]].
landing.aboutLead: I design *growth, identity and AI-powered* consumer experiences — and I care more about the problem than the pixels.
landing.about:
Across Microsoft Edge, Windows, Microsoft Search and automotive platforms, I've spent 11+ years turning complex, high-stakes problems into experiences people actually understand. Today I lead Growth Design for Edge — onboarding, activation, retention and personalization for millions of customers.

Before that, I shaped identity and authentication for billions of Windows sign-ins, advancing Microsoft's passwordless future. I work where product strategy, experimentation, systems thinking and AI innovation meet — partnering closely with Product, Engineering, Research, Data Science and Content.
landing.aboutSign: When I'm off the clock, I'm on a mountain bike — founder of a small riding community and a national cross-country racer.

work.title: Reimagining Windows Hello for a passwordless era
work.desc:
Windows Hello had become one of Microsoft's most important identity systems, but the experience had evolved incrementally over many years.

As Windows expanded across new devices, passkeys, security models, and authentication scenarios, the experience needed to feel more modern, more trustworthy, and easier to understand while preserving user confidence in a highly sensitive identity surface.

study.tagline: Designing a faster, more intuitive, and more trustworthy authentication experience for hundreds of millions of Windows users.
study.role: Product Designer II | Consumer Identity Experiences
study.scope: Modernizing Auth UX for Windows Hello and Credential UX

blocks.body: Result

blocks.heading: Windows Hello generates billions of authentication interactions every month

cells.heading: Adoption
cells.body:
• 423M Windows aMAD use Windows Hello
  • ~56% of Windows 11 devices sign in with Windows Hello
  • ~98.5% of Windows devices with an MSA use Windows Hello

cells.heading: Growth
cells.body:
• PIN usage growing 11.5% YoY
  • Biometric usage growing 13% YoY
  • Projected to reach ~800M MAD users in coming years

blocks.body: Windows Hello felt dated compared to other modern Windows experiences and competes trust experiences
As authentication became increasingly central to daily computing, the experience no longer reflected the quality users associated with modern trust and security systems.

blocks.right:
• Progressive disclosure
  • Smarter modality selection
  • Motion and transitions
  • Contextual interactions

items.body: "I am not real comfortable with putting my PIN in there."

blocks.heading: 38% of total reported issues are related to usability and craft of Windows Hello.
blocks.body: User misinterprets the title bar “Windows Security” and other security information impacting their confidence to move ahead in the flow.

items.body: "Windows Hello keeps using the wrong auth modality / How do I default Windows Hello to face every time?”

blocks.heading: Often unexpected auth methods are provided to users due to lack of system context.
blocks.body: Face verification when laptop lid is closed.

items.body: “Windows Hello, does not recognize my face and fingerprint every time”

blocks.heading: 52% of total reported issues are related to the performance and reliability of Windows Hello.
blocks.body: Face verification fails and exits to PIN even though camera is ON.

items.title: Authentication Didn't Feel Modern
items.body:
The Windows Hello animation and visual language had remained largely unchanged while authentication experiences across the industry continued to evolve.

A surface users interacted with repeatedly no longer conveyed the speed, trust, or polish expected from modern authentication experiences.

items.title: Designed for Systems. Not People.
items.body:
Over time, Credential UX accumulated accounts, authentication methods, credential providers, and security scenarios into a single experience.

People were being asked to make trust decisions without enough clarity, context, or control.

items.title: Reliability Was the User Experience
items.body:
Authentication is one of the few moments where users expect the system to work instantly and predictably.

Context mismatches, modality switching, and reliability issues created friction that undermined overall confidence.

blocks.heading: The opportunity wasn't simply to refresh a dialog.  It was to reimagine how Windows communicates trust.

items.title: Modernise the animations
items.body: Supported the brand and identity team

items.title: Rejuvinate the auth platform- Credential UX
items.body: This is what I worked on

items.title: Designed for reliability
items.body: Engineering area led by design influence

blocks.heading: Trust Was Hidden Behind Complexity
blocks.body: At the centre of Windows authentication sat Credential UI, a framework responsible for everything from passkeys and purchases to admin approvals and remote access. Over time it had evolved to support dozens of scenarios, but the experience still reflected system architecture more than user intent.

annotations.title: Title bar
annotations.body: Shows ‘”Windows security” branding which can be perceived as security threat

annotations.title: Title
annotations.body: Static title without any connection with the context shown below

annotations.title: Summary
annotations.body: Requires Immersive reading for crucial information

annotations.title: Active verification method
annotations.body: Required to be refreshed with the latest branding, WinUI guidelines and animation direction.

annotations.title: More than more choices
annotations.body: The menu consists of authentication methods, items unrelated to verification, user accounts, etc. See next

blocks.heading: More than "more choices"
blocks.body:
The menu consists of
  • Authentication methods
  • Items unrelated to verification
  • User accounts, etc.
  • No grouping or ranking logic

blocks.heading: Opportunity for Credential UX
blocks.body:
When people authenticate, they're trying to answer a few simple questions:

  • What am I doing?
  • Who is asking?
  • Is this safe?
  • What happens next?

The existing experience contained those answers, but often buried them inside dense text, generic titles, and low-value security information. Users had to read and interpret the dialog before they could make a confident decision.
blocks.editorName: When people authenticate, they're trying to answ…

items.title: Make context immediately understandable
items.body: Help users quickly understand what they're approving without needing to read dense summaries.

items.title: Make trust signals meaningful
items.body: Replace generic security information with cues that help users evaluate authenticity and safety.

items.title: Make important decisions glanceable
items.body: Surface the most relevant information first and reduce the effort required to build confidence.

blocks.heading: Exploring Trust & Authentication
blocks.body:
Before redesigning CredUI, I wanted to understand what makes authentication experiences feel trustworthy, effortless, and modern.

I studied authentication patterns across Windows, Passkeys, macOS, iOS, Android, and different CredUI scenarios to identify what users actually needed in moments of verification.

Modern authentication experiences:
  • Prioritize the most relevant information first
  • Use smart defaults
  • Provide contextual guidance
  • Reveal complexity progressively
  • Minimize decisions until necessary

blocks.heading: Turning Complexity into Context
blocks.body:
Across every authentication flow, the information users needed already existed. The challenge was that context was hidden inside dense text.

Instead of adding more information, I explored how to make trust instantly understandable through a single glanceable context object.

blocks.heading: Making Trust Glanceable
blocks.body:
Authentication isn't the decision.
Trust is.

blocks.left:
• Context hidden inside paragraphs
  • Users needed to read before understanding
  • Trust signals difficult to evaluate
  • Important information scattered across the dialog
blocks.right:
• Context condensed into a single glanceable object
  • Identity, destination, and intent surfaced upfront
  • Trust signals become immediately visible
  • Users understand what they're approving before authenticating

blocks.heading: Providing user choices
blocks.body:
Users could now understand what they were approving much faster. Once context became clear, another challenge surfaced:

How should users interact with authentication choices?

blocks.heading: Exploring a Reusable Interaction Model
blocks.body:
Across Passkeys, UAC, Remote Desktop and future authentication scenarios, users still needed ways to:
  • Switch authentication methods
  • Change accounts
  • Recover from failures
  • Access advanced options

blocks.heading: Exploring How Users Access Alternatives
blocks.body: Across Passkeys and UAC, we tested two interaction models.

cells.heading: Context-first with visible controls
cells.body: Keep account switching, authentication methods, and alternate devices easily discoverable from the main experience.

cells.heading: Context-first with progressive disclosure
cells.body: Keep the primary flow focused and reveal alternatives only when users actively seek them.

blocks.heading: Our learnings
blocks.body:
User testing consistently favoured Exploration A.

Participants found the dropdown object easier to discover and more intuitive because it aligned with familiar interaction patterns. Many naturally assumed it was the primary place to click when looking for options. Users repeatedly described it as easier to find, easier to understand, and easier to use.

items.body: “I feel like it's more intuitive in Flow A. I automatically know to click this object.”
“I’m more used to Flow A with the drop down menu as an easy indicator to click for more options.”
"A is easier. B looks better. I like that it says 'connect' instead of ok."

blocks.heading: The Context Object had become too successful.
blocks.body:
While users preferred Flow A, the study revealed something unexpected.

Users were deliberately clicking and exploring the object before engaging with authentication itself. The prominence of the card, avatar, dropdown affordances, and controls naturally pulled attention away from the primary task: verifying identity.

The Context Object was intended to provide understanding. Instead, it was becoming the focal point of the experience.

annotations.title: What users are doing
annotations.body:
Connect to a remote desktop,
Purchase an app,
Save a passkey,
Sign in to a website,
Access a protected resource

annotations.title: Role of Windows Hello
annotations.body: Windows Hello helps users understand what they were doing, trust the request, and complete their intended action with confidence.

annotations.title: Users want reassurance
annotations.body:
Before acting, users need answers to four questions:
 What am I trying to do?
 Why am I being asked to verify?
 Can I trust this request?
 Do I have the right controls if something is wrong?

blocks.heading: Design Decision
blocks.body:
Rather than optimizing for discoverability alone, we optimized for intent.
Authentication was the primary job to be done. Context should support that journey, not compete with it.
This led us to a framework where:

blocks.heading: That became the foundation for the final CredUI architecture.
From Framework to Architecture
blocks.body: The framework helped us move beyond redesigning screens and toward redesigning how authentication worked across Windows.

blocks.heading: Comparison between old and new experience

annotations.title: A new refreshed branding
annotations.body: Matches to the Windows security feature for building trust.

annotations.title: Context Before Authentication
annotations.body:
Make context the starting point of every authentication journey.
By transforming dense security information into a structured, glanceable object, users could immediately understand:

What they're doing
Who is requesting it
Which account is involved

annotations.title: Authentication Remains the Primary Task
annotations.body: Since authentication was the primary job to be done, the design intentionally reduced visual emphasis on alternate paths while keeping them accessible when needed.

annotations.title: Control Without Complexity
annotations.body:
Research showed users wanted control close at hand, but not competing for attention.
The final architecture kept alternatives discoverable without overwhelming the primary authentication flow.

blocks.heading: Control Without Complexity
blocks.body:
Authentication methods, credential providers, devices, and account switching were no longer mixed inside a single menu.

We separated core authentication choices from secondary actions, making the experience easier to understand while keeping advanced options accessible when needed.

This reduced cognitive load and helped users focus on the primary task: authentication.

blocks.heading: Security At A Glance
blocks.body:
Security information existed in the previous experience but required users to read and interpret it.

We shifted from text-heavy security messaging to status-driven communication.

When an action was safe, security information remained available but unobtrusive. When risk was present, severity became immediately visible through color, hierarchy, and contextual indicators.

Instead of asking users to read for danger, the experience made risk visible at a glance.

blocks.heading: One System Across Every Trust Moment
blocks.body: Although the scenarios were different, the underlying user needs were the same. The framework enabled a consistent architecture that could scale across Windows authentication experiences while adapting to each scenario's unique requirements.

blocks.heading: Ready for the passwordless future
blocks.body: Transformed Credential UX from a legacy authentication dialog into a scalable trust framework for Windows' passwordless future. The redesign created a scalable authentication architecture that could evolve with Windows' passwordless future.

items.title: Product Impact
items.body: Reimagined Windows' core authentication experience by transforming Credential UX from a dense, system-centric security dialog into a clearer, more glanceable trust experience that scales across scenarios.

items.title: Experience Impact
items.body: Introduced the Context Object, turning critical trust information from heavy text into a glanceable, reusable pattern that helped users quickly understand what they were approving and why.

items.title: Framework Impact
items.body: The framework informed how authentication, account switching, credential selection, and recovery experiences could work consistently across Windows scenarios.

items.title: Platform Impact
items.body: Created a scalable interaction model for Windows Hello as it evolved beyond device sign-in into a broader authentication platform supporting Passkeys, Recall, Admin Protection, Remote Desktop, Store Purchases and future passwordless experiences.

items.title: Scale Impact
items.body: Designed for one of Windows' most frequently used trust surfaces, supporting authentication across 423M+ active Windows devices and laying the foundation for future growth toward hundreds of millions more users as Windows accelerates toward a passwordless future.

work.title: One Microsoft, One Consent
work.desc: Microsoft’s consent strategy was moving from product-level permissions to an account-level model that could support connected, personalized experiences across the Microsoft ecosystem. My role was to translate that strategy into a scalable user experience framework, helping make consent clearer, more reusable, and consistent across product entry points like Windows OOBE, sign-in, apps, and contextual notices.

study.tagline: Creating a clearer, more consistent consent experience across Microsoft products, devices, and account journeys.
study.team: 1 Designer, 1 Product Manager, 3 Developers, 1 Content Designer, Partner product teams, Privacy and Compliances
study.scope: Account-level Privacy & Consent Platform across Microsoft Ecosystem

blocks.heading: The Problem
blocks.body:
Microsoft products increasingly worked together through a shared Microsoft account, yet consent was still requested independently across products.

Users encountered separate privacy notices and permission requests across Windows, Edge, Outlook, Teams, Office, and sign-in experiences, often being asked for similar permissions multiple times. The result wasn't just added friction. It fragmented trust by forcing users to make account-level decisions through disconnected product-level experiences.

items.caption: Windows 11 OOBE
Microsoft Edge
OneDrive
M365 Win32 apps
M365 Mobile apps

blocks.heading: Why It Mattered
blocks.body:
As Microsoft invested in more connected and personalized experiences, consent became a critical dependency.

Without a shared consent model, products had to request permissions independently, creating inconsistent experiences for users and making it harder for Microsoft teams to deliver seamless cross-product experiences at scale. The broader goal was to enable connected experiences across the Microsoft ecosystem while providing a scalable framework for evolving privacy and regulatory needs.

blocks.heading: Translating business strategy into an experience framework that could:
blocks.body:
• Help users understand the value behind consent
  • Create a consistent experience across Microsoft surfaces
  • Build user trust and confidence
  • Scale across products, devices, and future consent scenarios

items.heading: Create a One Microsoft experience
items.body: Show users that the value of consent extended across Microsoft products and devices, not just the product they were currently using.

items.heading: Turn consent into a value conversation
items.body: Explain why account-based data use could make Microsoft experiences more seamless, personalized, and useful.

items.heading: Inspire trust
items.body: Give users a sense of confidence and control so the experience felt transparent rather than transactional.

blocks.heading: How do we communicate value beyond text?
blocks.body: I tested whether product icons, account identity, and ecosystem cues could make the Microsoft account value proposition visible before users read the details.
blocks.editorName: How do we communicate value beyond text?

blocks.heading: How much content do we show?
blocks.body: I explored the line between transparency and cognitive load, especially because this was a compliance-heavy surface where too much copy could reduce comprehension.

blocks.heading: How do we present the content?
blocks.body: I explored whether Unified Consent should be framed as a product experience or a Microsoft account experience. This helped us evaluate how users interpret consent: as permission for a single app or as a relationship with their Microsoft account across products.

blocks.heading: Defining a unified Microsoft account messaging strategy
blocks.body: I led the design and synthesis of an evaluative research study that established how Microsoft account consent messaging should appear across Windows. The work aligned teams around a consistent interaction model, clarified the most effective delivery surfaces, and translated participant feedback into actionable cross-product design principles.

blocks.heading: Testing how much information to show upfront
blocks.body:
We compared a fully expanded notice with progressive disclosure to understand how users scanned, explored, and understood the message.


The chevron/accordion design serves two kinds of users (like to read or like to skim) and optimized for comprehension.

items.body: “It makes me want to actually read it because it is less daunting”
“I think Option B is the right amount of text that you can read and even stop on something that gets your attention”

blocks.body:
When we ask users: “do you think this message is about Word, Office, or Microsoft account?
  • Option B: 100%
  • Option A: 83%
When asked which was the least valuable item
  • “Recommendations just for you” ranked as the least valuable item

blocks.heading: Testing how visual treatment affects perceived ownership
blocks.body:
We compared an account-styled dialog with a product-native presentation to understand which treatment communicated the scope and source of the message more clearly.


The Microsoft account–styled dialog communicated account ownership more clearly.

items.body: “It really is what resembles for example, the Outlook log in which I know is also a box.”
items.cite: The scope of notice is clear

blocks.body:
Visual consistency with Microsoft account experiences helped users understand who was communicating and how broadly the message applied.
  • Users recognised the dialog from previous account and sign-in experiences.
  • 5 of 10 users expanded individual sections while reading through the message.

blocks.heading: From Notice to Identity
blocks.body: As Microsoft Account evolved toward a more unified identity, we shifted Unified Consent from a text-forward notice to a branded Microsoft Account experience. Introducing illustration and MSA visual language helped create continuity across sign-in, onboarding, and account management surfaces, reinforcing that this was an account-level experience rather than a standalone consent dialog.
Microsoft Account was undergoing a broader effort to create a more cohesive identity across sign-in, onboarding, account management, and privacy experiences.

blocks.heading: Evolving Beyond Disclosure
blocks.body:
As part of the broader Microsoft Account (MSA) redesign, the content team was defining a new communication language grounded in three principles:

  • Simplicity: Reduce cognitive effort and make information easier to understand.
  • Transparency: Clearly explain what is happening and why.
  • Value-Oriented Communication: Focus on the benefits users receive, not just what Microsoft is asking for.

blocks.left: The experience prioritized disclosure through expandable sections, requiring users to uncover information as they explored. Research showed experience was not easy comprehension and this conflicted with MSA principles of simplicity, transparency, and value-oriented communication.
blocks.right: The content team evolved the experience to prioritize comprehension. Through iterative testing and refinement, key messages were surfaced directly through clearer content, stronger visuals, and a cohesive One Microsoft narrative. Users no longer had to discover the value. They understood it immediately.

blocks.body: The goal was not simply to improve comprehension, but to create continuity across Microsoft Account touchpoints and help users recognize Unified Consent as part of the broader Microsoft Account journey.

blocks.heading: One framework. Many Microsoft experiences.
blocks.body: Consent comes to life

blocks.heading: Designing the Consent Framework
blocks.body:
What started as a solution to fragmented privacy notices evolved into a reusable consent framework. The platform enabled new regulatory and compliance scenarios to onboard without rebuilding consent experiences from scratch. The framework was easily bakeable to:

  • Win32 apps
  • Web apps
  • Mobile
  • MacOS
  • Windows OOBE

Unified Consent was to simplify how users granted permissions across Microsoft experiences. But as the framework matured, its value extended far beyond a single consent experience. It became an enabled future consent scenarios, including evolving regulatory requirements and region-specific experiences such as DMA, without requiring products to recreate consent experiences independently.

items.title: Product Impact
items.body: Created the experience framework for Microsoft’s Unified Consent strategy, replacing fragmented product-level consent experiences with a scalable account-level model across Microsoft surfaces.

items.title: Strategic Impact
items.body: Helped establish a reusable consent foundation that enabled Microsoft teams to deliver personalized cross-product experiences while adapting to evolving privacy and regulatory requirements.

items.title: Platform Impact
items.body: Contributed to the evolution of Unified Consent from a single consent notice into a platform powering Unified Consent, DMA compliance, Xbox privacy scenarios, and future Microsoft privacy experiences at billion-account scale.

blocks.heading: As of the Nov'24 MSA MAU Consented

work.title: Reinventing Edge Onboarding Journey
work.desc: After joining Microsoft Edge as the team's growth designer, I set out to rethink one of our most important customer moments: onboarding. What began as a design exploration evolved into a multi-year effort to redefine onboarding strategy, secure leadership investment, and build the next generation onboarding experience now in development.

study.tagline: Transforming onboarding from a setup flow into a growth platform for activation, retention, and long-term engagement.
study.role: Led onboarding vision, growth strategy, concept development, executive storytelling, product alignment, and final UX design for the next generation FRE
study.team: Design • PM • Engineering • Research • Content Design • Privacy • Data Science
study.scope: New Device First Run Experience • Activation • Retention • Personalization • Consent • Data Import

blocks.body: Why Onboarding Matters?

blocks.heading: Onboarding as a growth challenge
blocks.body:
I discovered Edge's onboarding challenge was fundamentally a growth challenge.

Different users entered Edge with dramatically different goals:

  • MVUs wanted speed
  • Edge PBs needed retention
  • Dual-browser users needed conversion
  • Local-account users needed MSA adoption
  • Dormant users needed re-engagement

Onboarding wasn't just helping users finish setup. It was helping them move up the Edge growth ladder.

blocks.heading: Modern Onboarding
blocks.body: While studying onboarding patterns across Windows OOBE and modern browsers, I noticed a common trend: successful onboarding experiences focused less on setup and more on helping users experience value quickly.

items.caption: Drives MSA sign-in, increases ecosystem retention, and makes switching costs higher once users are connected across devices.
Lowers adoption barriers by allowing users to keep using Chrome while gradually transitioning to Edge.
Accelerates browser switching, improves activation, and increases likelihood of long-term Edge adoption.
Expands data signals that power personalization, engagement, retention, and advertising opportunities.
Builds emotional attachment and strengthens product affinity through customization.
Increases satisfaction and reduces the likelihood that users abandon the experience because it feels unfamiliar
Creates habit loops, improves launch frequency, and increases daily engagement.

blocks.body:
The existing onboarding experience had evolved organically over time.

Sign-in, consent, import, personalization, pinning and activation were spread across disconnected surfaces.

Users experienced onboarding as separate experiences rather than a coherent journey. This contributed to drop-off between stages and made future growth initiatives increasingly difficult to scale.

cells.heading: Hard surface
cells.body:
Ensure critical setup and compliance actions happen before users start browsing.
  • Sign-in
  • Sync notice
  • Data import
  • Privacy consents

cells.heading: High-priority actions because they directly impacted:
cells.body:
• User retention
  • Browser switching success
  • Compliance requirements
  • Microsoft account adoption

cells.heading: Soft surface
cells.body:
Drive personalization and activation without introducing excessive friction.

  • Themes
  • Layout preferences
  • Pinning
  • New tab customization

cells.heading: Important but not critical
cells.body: These experiences were considered not critical enough to block users. They therefore lived in a separate, lighter-weight onboarding experience after the mandatory setup moments.

blocks.heading: My viewpoint
blocks.body: I began to question whether onboarding should be organized around business priorities or customer psychology. The most delightful and identity-building experiences were treated as secondary steps, despite being the moments most likely to create confidence, preference, and retention.

items.caption: High business-value actions such as import and consent were surfaced early and prominently, while personalization experiences were deferred into a secondary onboarding flow.  My hypothesis was that personalization was not a secondary experience. It was one of the earliest opportunities to help users feel ownership, confidence, and attachment to Edge.

items.body: Fragmented in multiple surfaces - Hard / Soft increasing the overall step counts
Multiple screens with similar objectives - Consents / Customize
Prioritizes consents (blocking overlays) over personalization, and other growth opportunities
Follows outdated pattern and can be perceived as distracting or following dark patterns

items.title: Surface Unification
items.body: A single continuous onboarding journey

items.title: Layout Unification
items.body:
Create a common onboarding language.

Shared:
  • visual structure
  • navigation model
  • progress model
  • CTA placement
  • interaction patterns

The goal was to make every step feel like part of the same journey regardless of content.

items.title: Consent Unification
items.body: Rather than treating consent as standalone interruptions, integrate them into the narrative of onboarding.

blocks.heading: Most ambitious directional concepts
blocks.body:
The challenge was reimagining how onboarding should feel as a single cohesive journey.

Rather than jumping straight into a solution, I explored multiple future directions that challenged existing assumptions around surfaces, immersion, personalization, and activation.

After exploring the breadth of possibilities, a clear pattern emerged across every successful direction:

  • One onboarding journey
  • One narrative
  • One visual language
  • One place where users configure Edge

The question was no longer whether onboarding should be unified.

The question became:

What is the most compelling version of a unified onboarding experience?

blocks.heading: Future of Unified Onboarding
blocks.body:
With alignment forming around unification, I shifted focus from exploring different surfaces to exploring the full potential of a unified onboarding experience.

The goal was no longer to redesign individual onboarding screens.

The goal was to rethink what onboarding could become if every critical setup, personalization, and activation moment lived within a single continuous journey.

Rather than treating onboarding as separate experiences owned by different teams, the Unified FRE vision proposed a single onboarding framework where onboarding, activation, personalization, consent, and habit formation worked together as one cohesive system.

The goal wasn't simply to merge screens.

The goal was to create a continuous journey that helped users understand, personalize, and adopt Edge from day one.
Once leadership aligned on the opportunity, the work evolved from exploration into product development. This required translating the vision into something that could actually ship.

items.title: One Surface
items.body: Users should experience onboarding as a single continuous journey rather than multiple disconnected flows.

items.title: Progressive Value
items.body: Users should continuously discover value while onboarding instead of completing setup before seeing benefits.

items.title: Reduce Effort
items.body: The best onboarding experiences remove future work rather than create additional tasks, reducing friction beyond onboarding.

items.title: Easy Experimentation
items.body: Easy to reassembled, reordered, and optimize for the best business outcomes without sacrificing user satisfaction.

blocks.heading: Compromises & Reality
blocks.body:
The final experience wasn't a direct translation of the original vision.

Privacy requirements, engineering constraints, experimentation needs, and rollout realities all shaped the final product.

My role evolved from generating future-state concepts to helping teams identify which ideas delivered the most value while remaining feasible to ship.

blocks.heading: Compliance
blocks.body: Rather than treating compliance as isolated interruptions, the new framework worked well integrating world wide compliance requirements, reducing fragmentation and preserving flow continuity.

blocks.heading: Responsive by Default
blocks.body: Framework was designed to scale across different window states, device configurations, and future onboarding modules, ensuring a consistent experience while supporting future extensibility.

blocks.heading: Motion with Purpose
blocks.body: Aligned with MAI motion specifications, ensuring onboarding shared the same interaction language as other Microsoft AI experiences. Motion wasn't treated as decoration, but as part of the system that created continuity, trust, and familiarity across products.

blocks.heading: Designing for Learning
blocks.body: Rather than hard-coding a single onboarding narrative, Unified FRE was designed as a framework capable of testing different theories about what drives adoption.

cells.heading: Setup First
cells.body: • Sign In → Lightning Import → Themes → Consent → Pinning

cells.heading: Traditional onboarding philosophy
cells.body:
• Establish identity first
  • Bring browsing data into Edge
  • Complete required setup
  • Personalize later

cells.heading: What we wanted to learn
cells.body:
• Does reducing switching friction upfront increase onboarding completion?
  • Does completing setup tasks early improve activation?
  • Are users more likely to continue when onboarding begins with familiar migration actions?

cells.heading: Delight First
cells.body: • Pinning → Themes → Sign In → Lightning Import → Consent

cells.heading: Tying up to the vision
cells.body: • Instead of starting with account setup and migration, onboarding begins by showing users the value of personalizing Edge.

cells.heading: What we wanted to learn
cells.body:
• Does leading with delight increase engagement and completion?
  • Do users form stronger impressions of Edge when personalization comes first?
  • Can value-building moments create enough momentum to carry users through later setup requirements?
  • Should onboarding optimize for excitement and ownership before configuration?

blocks.body: Should onboarding ask users to invest in Edge first, or should Edge invest in the user first?
blocks.sub: We are yet to get the answers...

items.title: Product Impact
items.body: Increased TAM of Edge's most engaging onboarding moments, reducing onboarding complexity from 7 steps to 4 and simplifying consent collection from 3 screens to 1.

items.title: Strategic Impact
items.body: Redefined onboarding for a surface experienced by 25M+ users monthly, transforming FRE from disconnected setup flows into a platform for activation, personalization, and retention.

items.title: Leadership Impact
items.body: Drove the initiative from early exploratory concepts through leadership alignment, product strategy, and a production-ready experience now moving into development.

work.title: Designing Growth Lifecycle for Xbox Customers
work.desc: Designed a growth lifecycle vision for Xbox Game Pass spanning acquisition, engagement, retention, and reactivation across Windows and web experiences. The work uncovered new opportunities to reduce churn through personalization, strengthen long-term engagement, and guide future product investments beyond traditional subscription management.

study.tagline: A strategic vision for how Xbox could build stronger relationships with gamers across every stage of the subscription journey.
study.role: Product Designer II | Leading the vision
study.team: 3 Designers, 1 Product Manager, 1 Content Designer, 4 Engineer, 1 Data Science
study.scope: Growth · Acquisition · Retention · Churn Reduction · Re-activation · Personalization · Platform Strategy

cells.body: Xbox Game Pass was growing rapidly, but customer experiences were optimized around individual subscription scenarios such as acquisition, management, upgrades, and cancellation. The broader customer journey was fragmented, making it difficult to identify opportunities that spanned the entire lifecycle.

• Mapped the end-to-end Game Pass customer lifecycle.
  • Developed the strategic vision for acquisition, retention, and reactivation.
  • Defined personalization principles for future subscription experiences.
  • Created north-star concepts that informed future execution and planning.

items.title: What is Xbox Game Pass
items.body: Xbox Game Pass is Microsoft's gaming subscription service that provides access to a catalogue of games and benefits across Xbox, PC, and cloud gaming experiences. The service includes several subscription tiers and membership benefits.

items.title: Who are the users?
items.body:
Xbox gamers at different stages of their subscription journey:

  • New and prospective subscribers
  • Active Game Pass members
  • At-risk subscribers
  • Cancelled subscribers
  • Returning subscribers

items.title: Windows objectives
items.body:
Retain existing and win new customers with leading silicon, device, and platform capabilities.
And Inspire customers to complete more jobs on windows with Microsoft+ services and experiences.

blocks.heading: The team was primarily focused on subscription management scenarios, particularly reducing cancellations and improving save experiences when users attempted to leave the service.
blocks.caption: Benefits shown based on GamePass subscription | Static contents, lack of personalization

cells.heading: We treated cancellation as a single problem.
cells.body:
After examining customer behaviour, it became clear that people left for fundamentally different reasons:

  • Cost sensitivity
  • Lack of time
  • Lack of relevant content
  • Forgotten renewals
  • Temporary disengagement

The real problem wasn't cancellation itself.

The real problem was that we weren't helping different users recognize and rediscover the value relevant to them.

blocks.heading: How I Changed the Strategy
blocks.left: • How do we stop customers from cancelling?
blocks.right: • How do we create value throughout the customer lifecycle?

items.caption: I’m a long time gamer by myself and the topic was something very close to my personality. I stepped back and mapped the entire Xbox customer lifecycle to identify where Microsoft could create value beyond subscription retention.
The cancellation problem wasn't a single problem.  Different users cancelled for different reasons: Cost, Lack of content, No time to play, Forgotten renewals
Not all churn is equal. Different churn types represent different recovery opportunities, and each opportunity requires a different business strategy.
I reframed the work from "prevent cancellation" to "help different gamer types recognize value in different ways."   This shift influenced the direction of future exploration and investment.

cells.heading: This shift expanded the conversation beyond cancellation and surfaced new opportunities in:
cells.body:
• Acquisition
  • Engagement
  • Retention
  • Family growth
  • Re-subscription
  • Customer reactivation

"How do we stop customers from cancelling?"

to

"How do we create value throughout the customer lifecycle?"

Rather than designing a screen, I was helping define where the product should go next.

blocks.heading: The vision was guided by five core principles:

items.title: Personalize the experience
items.body: Different players have different motivations and should see experiences tailored to their interests, gaming history, and needs.

items.title: Reinforce value
items.body: Help users understand the benefits, savings, content, and experiences available through their membership.

items.title: Celebrate progress
items.body: Highlight achievements, milestones, and gaming history to strengthen emotional connection with the service.

items.title: Offer flexibility
items.body: Provide alternatives beyond cancellation such as plan changes, pauses, or different subscription options.

items.title: Design for growth
items.body: Create modular experiences that allow future experimentation and expansion across channels and touchpoints.

blocks.heading: How do we recover revenue differently for different types of churn?

cells.heading: Value led Churn
cells.body:
Users questioned economic value.

  • Can't afford it
  • Already played available games

cells.heading: Engagement Churn
cells.body:
Users no longer saw reasons to stay.

  • Not enough interesting games
  • Prefer another gaming service

cells.heading: Operational Churn
cells.body:
Users no longer saw reasons to stay.

  • Not enough interesting games
  • Prefer another gaming service

cells.heading: Business solution:
cells.body:
• Pause plans
  • Flexible subscriptions
  • Future game notifications
This is where retention becomes product strategy rather than UX optimization.

cells.heading: Business solution:
cells.body:
• Personalized recommendations
  • Better discovery
  • Upcoming catalogue visibility
This shifts the conversation from subscription management to content marketing.

cells.heading: Business solution:
cells.body:
• Personalized recommendations
  • Better discovery
  • Upcoming catalogue visibility
This shifts the conversation from subscription management to content marketing.

blocks.heading: How do we turn existing Windows customers into Xbox Game Pass customers?
blocks.body:
Windows was already:

  • High traffic
  • Trusted
  • Account-centric
  • Biggest canvass of Microsoft, and connected to Microsoft's ecosystem

Making it a natural entry point for Xbox growth.

blocks.heading: Identified Directions
blocks.left:
• Interruptive
  • Feels like a promotion or advertisement
  • Good for awareness
  • Limited space to explain value
  • One-time interaction
  • Generic experience
  • Risk of being perceived as noise
blocks.right:
• Intent-driven
  • A natural extension of the OS
  • Good for discovery, education, comparison
  • Can showcase benefits, plans leading to conversions
  • Supports the entire lifecycle
  • Can leverage account context and subscription status for personalization

items.title: Cancellation & Retention
items.body: Transformed cancellation from a subscription-management challenge into a customer lifecycle strategy spanning retention, re-engagement, and long-term customer value growth.

items.title: Acquisition
items.body: Positioned Windows as a strategic Xbox growth channel, defining a vision that connected acquisition, onboarding, retention, and reactivation through account-centric.

items.title: Mentoring & Shipping
items.body: Scaled impact by mentoring two junior designers and translating strategic vision into executable MVPs that informed experimentation and shipped customer experiences.

blocks.heading: The vision landed
blocks.body: The final experience evolved from a downgrade-focused cancellation flow into a content-led retention experience. Rather than immediately presenting alternative plans, the experience highlights personalized game recommendations, recently added titles, upcoming releases, and key membership benefits to help subscribers rediscover the value of Game Pass before making a cancellation decision.

items.caption: Cancellation flow more personalised and value oriented
Windows Settings - Subscription management in Accounts settings
Windows Settings - Subscription card in Settings home

work.title: Growth and retention for Game Pass
work.desc: Led growth and retention design for Xbox Game Pass — turning acquisition, cancellation and lifecycle moments into experiments that helped people see the value and stay.

study.tagline: Turning cancellation moments into reasons to stay.

blocks.heading: The subscription is the product — so is leaving it.
blocks.body: I led growth and retention experience work for Xbox Game Pass across acquisition, plan evaluation, cancellation and the lifecycle in between — designing the moments that decide whether a player subscribes, stays, or comes back.
list: Led growth and retention experience work for Game Pass
Redesigned the cancellation and lifecycle journeys
Ran experimentation-led retention interventions
Partnered with Data Science on every bet

blocks.body: Most products treat cancellation as a door. It is actually the most honest conversation you will ever have with a customer.
blocks.sub: The moment someone tries to leave is the moment they tell you exactly what value they are missing.

items.title: Meet doubt with value
items.body: At cancellation, surface the value the customer has not used yet — not a guilt trip.

items.title: Experiment, do not assume
items.body: Treat retention as a series of measured bets with Data Science, not opinions.

items.title: Fix the fundamentals
items.body: Make plan evaluation and acquisition legible so people choose with confidence.

items.a: A subscription that gives players a rotating library of games for a monthly fee.
Everything from signing up, to evaluating plans, to pausing or cancelling.
It is the highest-signal, highest-leverage moment to understand and retain a customer.

items.caption: Cancellation and lifecycle flows for Game Pass. Visuals abstracted; shipping UI is confidential.
Plan-evaluation and retention-intervention explorations.

left: Cancellation treated as a dead end
right: Cancellation reframed as a value conversation

blocks.body: Retention is not a dark pattern. It is making the value you already deliver impossible to overlook.
blocks.sub: If I had more time: feed the cancellation insights back into acquisition and onboarding.

work.title: Onboarding that earns the second session
work.desc: Owning the strategy and design of Edge's First Run Experience — the browser's most critical acquisition and activation journey. Setup, imports, personalization and retention, designed end-to-end and validated through experimentation.

study.tagline: The browser's most important ten minutes — designed to earn the second session.
study.team: PM · Engineering · Research · Data Science · Content

blocks.heading: The first run is the whole relationship, compressed.
blocks.body: Edge's First Run Experience is the first thing tens of millions of people see after choosing — or being nudged toward — a new browser. It's the highest-leverage moment in the entire growth funnel: setup, sign-in, import, personalization and the first taste of value all happen before anyone has decided to stay. I own the strategy and design for this journey end-to-end.
list: Framing the first run as an activation problem, not a setup wizard
Designing the flow from launch → import → personalize → first value
Partnering with Data Science to instrument and read every step
Running the experiments that decide what ships

blocks.heading: Small changes to the first minute move millions of sessions.

items.a: The very first session after install — the setup and onboarding flow, before someone starts browsing normally.

items.q: Why does it matter so much?
items.a: It's the single most-seen surface in the product and the biggest lever on whether a new user returns for a second session.
A new user reaching real value — signed in, set up and actually using the browser — not just having installed it.

blocks.body: A setup flow optimized for completion isn't the same as an onboarding designed for retention.
blocks.sub: Every extra step in the first minute is paid for in people who never come back.

blocks.kicker: What we got wrong at first
blocks.body: The instinct on a setup flow is to ask for everything up front — sign in, import, pin, set defaults — because each one is "good for the user." But stacking asks in the first minute quietly trades the second session for a more complete first one. We had to reframe the goal from "finish setup" to "earn the next visit."

blocks.heading: Three moves that reframed onboarding as growth.

items.title: Sequence for momentum
items.body: Order the asks so the fastest path to value comes first, and defer everything that can wait until the browser has already proven useful.

items.title: Make value legible early
items.body: Show, don't tell — surface a real, personal reason to keep the browser open inside the first session rather than promising benefits later.

items.title: Instrument every step
items.body: Treat the flow as a measurable funnel with Data Science, so each screen earns its place through experimentation, not opinion.

items.caption: Flow architecture — launch → import → personalize → first value. Visuals abstracted; shipping UI is confidential.
Sequencing explorations for the first-minute asks.

blocks.heading: From a checklist to a welcome.
blocks.left:
• Setup optimized for completion, not retention
  • Asks stacked into the first minute
  • Success measured at "finished setup"
blocks.right:
• Onboarding sequenced to earn the second session
  • Value shown before friction is introduced
  • Success measured on activation and return

blocks.body: The best onboarding doesn't feel like onboarding. It feels like the product being immediately worth it.
blocks.sub: If I had more time: push personalization earlier, and close the loop from first-run choices to week-four retention.

work.title: A passwordless future for billions of sign-ins
work.desc: Led UX strategy for foundational Windows passkey experiences across Windows Hello, Microsoft Account and FIDO — reducing authentication friction while strengthening trust. Established scalable patterns adopted across identity journeys.

study.tagline: Making the safest way to sign in also the easiest.
study.team: Design · Engineering · Security · Product · Compliance · Standards

blocks.heading: Making the safest way to sign in also the easiest.
blocks.body: I owned UX for passwordless sign-in across Windows Hello, Microsoft Account, FIDO and Passkeys — authentication that has to feel effortless while protecting billions of sign-ins. The goal: make the most secure option the one people actually reach for.
list: Led UX for foundational Windows passkey experiences
Advanced passwordless across Windows Hello, Microsoft Account and FIDO
Designed third-party credential-manager support for Windows
Represented Microsoft at the FIDO Alliance and Authenticate 2024

blocks.heading: Security that reaches the whole Windows ecosystem.

items.label: passkeys enrolled in the first 10 days

items.a: A phishing-resistant sign-in bound to your device — nothing to type, remember or leak.
The moment you grant an app access to your Microsoft account — standardized into one clear pattern across the ecosystem.
Passwords are the single biggest source of account compromise; removing them lifts security and cuts friction at the same time.

blocks.body: Security and ease usually pull in opposite directions. Passwordless only wins if the safe path is also the fast one.
blocks.sub: At billions of sign-ins, one confusing step becomes millions of failed logins and support calls.

blocks.heading: Turning a security mandate into an experience people choose.

items.title: Design for trust, not just tasks
items.body: Make the safer choice legible and reassuring at the exact moment of decision.

items.title: Establish reusable patterns
items.body: Define authentication patterns that scale across Windows Hello, passkeys and consent journeys.

items.title: Partner with standards
items.body: Work across Security, Compliance and the FIDO Alliance so the UX and the spec evolve together.

items.caption: Passkey enrollment and sign-in flows across Windows. Visuals abstracted; shipping UI is confidential.
Unified Consent patterns standardized across the Microsoft account ecosystem.

blocks.heading: From passwords to passkeys.
left: Passwords — phishable, forgettable, costly to support
right: Phishing-resistant passkeys as a first-class choice

blocks.body: The best security disappears — you feel safe without being asked to think about it.
blocks.sub: If I had more time: push passkey education earlier, and close the loop from enrollment to long-term retention.

work.title: Finding the answer, not just the results
work.desc: Designed AI-powered search and discovery across Outlook, Teams, SharePoint, Bing and Microsoft 365 — intelligent answers that help people find information faster, with accessibility and platform consistency built in.

study.tagline: Finding the answer, not just a list of results.
study.team: Design · Engineering · Research · Content · Data Science

blocks.heading: Answers, in the flow of work.
blocks.body: I led UX for Microsoft Search Answer experiences across Outlook, Teams, SharePoint, Bing and Microsoft 365 — turning search from a list of links into direct, trustworthy answers that meet people where they already work.
list: Led UX for Microsoft Search Answer experiences
Shipped Acronym, Calendar, Link, Knowledge and People answers
Standardized people and profile-card experiences
Championed inclusive, accessible design across surfaces

blocks.body: Search that returns ten blue links makes people do the work the system should have done for them.
blocks.sub: In the enterprise, the answer is often a person, a policy or a definition — not a webpage.

items.title: Model the question types
items.body: Identify the recurring things people actually ask — acronyms, people, events — and answer them directly.

items.title: Design for confidence
items.body: Show the source and context so people trust the answer without leaving the page.

items.title: Make it consistent
items.body: Define patterns that read the same across Outlook, Teams, SharePoint and Bing.

items.a: A direct, structured response — a definition, a person card, a meeting — instead of a link to go read.
Answers that only appear when confidence is high, staying out of the way otherwise.
Search is the front door to work — it has to work for everyone, on every surface.

items.caption: Answer patterns — Acronym, Calendar, Link, Knowledge and People. Visuals abstracted; shipping UI is confidential.
People and profile-card standardization across Microsoft 365.

left: Results lists that offload work to the user
Answers that interrupt more than they help

blocks.body: A great answer respects your attention — it shows up when it is sure, and gets out of the way when it is not.
blocks.sub: If I had more time: expand answer coverage and tune confidence thresholds per surface.

work.title: Interfaces for the road, at premium standard
work.desc: Designed next-generation infotainment, connected-vehicle and in-vehicle interaction experiences — HMI concepts, interaction models and production-ready validation for premium automotive platforms.

study.tagline: Interfaces for the road, at premium standard.
study.team: Design · Engineering · User Research · OEM partners

blocks.heading: Designing for a screen you can only half look at.
blocks.body: Across Jaguar Land Rover and Tata Elxsi, I designed next-generation infotainment and connected-vehicle experiences for premium automotive platforms — from HMI concepts and interaction models through to production-ready validation.
list: Designed next-generation infotainment and connected-vehicle experiences
Led HMI concepts, interaction models and design validation
Delivered production-ready designs for premium platforms
Partnered closely with product, engineering and user research

blocks.body: In a car, the interface competes with the one task that actually matters: driving.
blocks.sub: Every glance has a cost measured in metres — clarity is not a nicety, it is safety.

items.title: Reduce glance time
items.body: Structure information so a driver can understand it in a fraction of a second.

items.title: Model the whole journey
items.body: Design interaction models across infotainment and connected-vehicle contexts, not single screens.

items.title: Validate for production
items.body: Work with engineering to take concepts through to production-ready, testable designs.

items.caption: Infotainment and connected-vehicle HMI concepts. Visuals abstracted; client work is confidential.
Interaction models and production-ready validation.

left: Interfaces that demand too much attention

blocks.body: Automotive taught me that the highest form of design is the one you barely notice while it keeps you safe.
blocks.sub: It is the same instinct I bring to growth and identity today: clarity at the moment that matters.
